Solomon encourages wealthy people to do good for others. Give freely, even if it seems wasted or lost. Give to many. Do not excuse yourself with the good you have done from the good you still need to do. It is not lost, but well invested. We should expect trouble, because we are born into it; it is wise to do good when things are prosperous. Riches cannot benefit us if we do not help others. Every person must strive to be a blessing in the place where God’s providence has put them. Wherever we are, we can find worthwhile work to do if we have the willingness to do it. If we exaggerate every small difficulty, raise objections, and imagine hardships, we will never progress, let alone finish our work. Storms and trials are, in God's hands, meant to test us. God’s actions will align with his word, whether we see it or not. And we can trust God to provide for us without our anxious worries. Do not become discouraged in doing good, because at the right time, in God’s time, you will receive a reward.

Life is enjoyable for bad people, because they find their satisfaction in this life; it is enjoyable for good people, because it is a time of preparation for something better; it is enjoyable for everyone. Here is a warning to think about death even when life is most enjoyable. Solomon makes an impactful speech to young people. They will desire opportunities to pursue every pleasure. Then follow your desires, but be sure that God will call you to judgment. How many indulge in every craving and rush into every sinful pleasure! But God records every one of their sinful thoughts and desires, their idle words and wicked words. If they want to avoid regret and fear, if they want hope and comfort on a dying bed, if they want to escape misery here and in the future, let them remember the emptiness of youthful pleasures. It is clear that Solomon intends to condemn the pleasures of sin. His goal is to lead young people toward purer and more lasting joys. This is not the language of someone begrudging youthful pleasures because they can no longer enjoy them; but of someone who has, by a miracle of mercy, been brought back safely. He wants to dissuade the young from trying a path from which so few return. If young people want to live a life of true happiness, if they want to secure happiness in the future, let them remember their Creator during their youth.
